lesson: PLA strung at 215C in moderate humidity. Dropping nozzle to ~200C and nudging retraction up cleared it. With PLA, temperature is usually the bigger lever than retraction distance.
High ambient humidity made PLA string even at a sane 205C. Settings tweaks did little; the real fix was drying the filament. Above ~60% humidity, suspect moisture before chasing retraction.
Steep PLA overhang printed clean with cooling fan at 100%. PLA likes maximum cooling on overhangs to freeze the layer before it droops.

PLA overhang sagged in a warm room even at full fan. Warm ambient air weakens part cooling; on hot days drop a few degrees and ensure unobstructed airflow, or add support past ~50 degrees of overhang.
Short PLA bridge under ~50mm printed flat with full cooling and slightly increased bridge flow. Max fan plus brisk bridge speed is the reliable combo for PLA spans.
First layer wouldn't stick in a cold room. Bumping bed to ~60C, slowing the first layer, and turning the fan off for the opening layers fixed adhesion. Cold ambient hurts first-layer bonding most.
PETG strung badly at 245C. Lowering to ~232C, raising travel speed, and enabling wipe-before-travel cleaned it up. PETG strings more than PLA by nature, so live at the low end of its temp range.
PETG is hygroscopic and strung heavily in humid air despite good settings. Drying the spool was the only durable fix. Above ~60% humidity, dry PETG first and treat settings as secondary.
PETG overhang drooped when I ran the fan low for strength. PETG needs a cooling compromise: ~30-50% fan generally, bumped to 60-70% specifically over overhangs and bridges.
PETG layers came out brittle with the fan too high. Dropping cooling to ~30% and raising temp 5C restored layer bonding. Over-cooling PETG causes delamination; it is the opposite of PLA.
PETG bridge held by temporarily raising fan to ~70% for the span only, then returning to 40%. Targeted cooling bursts beat globally high fan, which would weaken the rest of the part.

Clean PETG print at 230C with gentle retraction (moderate distance, ~40mm/s) in dry conditions. PETG stays molten longer than PLA, so gentle retraction plus fast travel beats aggressive retraction, which clogs.
ABS corners lifted in an open, cool room. ABS needs an enclosure and a 100-110C bed; without it, warping is near-certain regardless of settings.
ABS first layer held with bed at 105C, brim, and fan off for opening layers. Draught control matters more than glue for ABS adhesion.
TPU strung badly; it is highly hygroscopic. Dry the spool and print slow (~25mm/s) with minimal retraction — TPU clogs with aggressive retraction.
PETG strings heavily in humid conditions. Dry filament at 65C for 4+ hours, then lower nozzle temp 5-10C and increase retraction to 5-6mm. Wet PETG is the #1 cause of stringing.
PETG bridges cleanly with fan at 80%, nozzle 240C, and retraction at 5mm. PETG likes more cooling than people think — just not on the first layer.
PLA overhangs sag in a hot room (31C ambient). Max fan (100%) and lower nozzle to 200C rescued the next print. PLA needs aggressive cooling when ambient is above 28C.
PLA vase mode at 205C/60C bed with 100% fan produced a clean spiral. PLA is forgiving in cool, dry rooms — the baseline material for vase-mode prints.
ABS overhangs curled upward at 80% fan — ABS hates part cooling. Keep fan at 0-20% for ABS, rely on enclosure ambient temp instead. Fan on ABS causes layer delamination.
TPU printed clean with 3mm retraction at 25mm/s in a dry room. TPU's flexibility means retraction must be slow and short — fast retraction stretches the filament instead of pulling it back.
PETG adhesion is strong on PEI at 80C bed — too strong on smooth PEI without release agent. Use textured PEI or glue stick as a release layer, not an adhesion aid, for PETG.
My own PETG print at 240C nozzle came out very stringy on a retraction-heavy part. PETG strings when the nozzle runs hot or the spool is damp - dry the spool, drop ~10C, and increase retraction.

Print detachment detected when layer-to-layer NRMSE score > 1.0 AND 5-layer deviance > 1.0. Large structural changes across consecutive layers indicate the part has separated from the bed. Check bed adhesion: clean surface, raise bed temp, use brim or raft.

Filament runout or nozzle clog detected when NRMSE score < 0.2 AND deviance < 0.2. Near-zero structural change means no material is being deposited. Check filament spool, extruder tension, and nozzle for clogs.

Partial breakage detected when frame-to-frame score delta > 0.2 AND deviance delta > 0.2. Sudden structural changes mid-print indicate layer delamination or part fracture. Reduce cooling fan, increase nozzle temp, check for drafts.
